title: Frontend Mastery — JS · React · Next.js · Angular
body:
The Frontend Mastery Bible

JavaScript React 19 Next.js 16 Angular 21

Every concept · every detail · every interview question — beginner to master

JavaScript is the language of the web. Master these fundamentals and everything else becomes easier.

Types & Type Coercion

JavaScript has 8 data types. 7 primitives + 1 non-primitive:

// 7 Primitives (immutable, stored by value)
typeof "hello"     // "string"
typeof 42          // "number" (includes integers, floats, NaN, Infinity)
typeof 42n         // "bigint"
typeof true        // "boolean"
typeof undefined   // "undefined"
typeof Symbol()    // "symbol"
typeof null        // "object" ← BUG! null is a primitive, this is a JS quirk

// 1 Non-primitive (mutable, stored by reference)
typeof {}          // "object" — includes arrays, functions, dates, etc.
typeof []          // "object" — use Array.isArray() to check
typeof function(){} // "function" — special object subtype

Type Coercion — The Source of All Bugs

// == (loose equality) coerces types
"5" == 5        // true  (string → number)
false == 0      // true  (boolean → number)
null == undefined // true  (special rule)
"" == 0         // true  (string → number)

// === (strict equality) — NO coercion, ALWAYS use this
"5" === 5       // false
false === 0     // false

// Falsy values (all evaluate to false in boolean context)
// false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN
// EVERYTHING else is truthy (including "0", "false", [], {})
== is like asking "do these look similar?" === is like asking "are these exactly the same thing?" Always use === unless you have a very specific reason not to.

var vs let vs const

// var — function-scoped, hoisted with undefined, can be redeclared
console.log(x); // undefined (hoisted!)
var x = 5;
var x = 10; // no error — can redeclare

// let — block-scoped, hoisted but NOT initialized (TDZ)
console.log(y); // ReferenceError! (Temporal Dead Zone)
let y = 5;
// let y = 10; // SyntaxError — cannot redeclare

// const — block-scoped, must be initialized, cannot reassign
const z = 5;
// z = 10; // TypeError — cannot reassign

// BUT: const objects/arrays CAN be mutated!
const user = { name: 'Alice' };
user.name = 'Bob'; // ✅ This works! Only the binding is const.
// user = {}; // ❌ TypeError — cannot reassign the variable
Rule: Default to const. Use let only when you need to reassign. Never use var.

The Event Loop — How JS Runs

JavaScript is single-threaded but non-blocking thanks to the event loop.

console.log('1');                    // Sync → Call Stack

setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0); // Macro task → Task Queue

Promise.resolve().then(() =>
  console.log('3')                    // Micro task → Microtask Queue
);

console.log('4');                    // Sync → Call Stack

// Output: 1, 4, 3, 2
// WHY: Sync first (1,4) → Microtasks (3) → Macro tasks (2)
The event loop is like a restaurant kitchen. The chef (call stack) handles orders one at a time. If an order needs the oven (async), it goes in and the chef starts the next order. When the oven dings (callback), the order goes back on the counter (task queue) and waits for the chef to be free. Microtasks (Promises) are like VIP orders — they always jump ahead of the regular queue.

Reference vs Value

// Primitives — copied by VALUE
let a = 5;
let b = a;   // b gets a COPY of 5
b = 10;
console.log(a); // 5 (unchanged)

// Objects — copied by REFERENCE
let obj1 = { name: 'Alice' };
let obj2 = obj1; // obj2 points to SAME object
obj2.name = 'Bob';
console.log(obj1.name); // 'Bob' (CHANGED! Same reference)

// How to copy objects
const shallow = { ...obj1 };           // spread (shallow copy)
const shallow2 = Object.assign({}, obj1); // Object.assign
const deep = structuredClone(obj1);    // deep copy (modern)
const deep2 = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj1)); // deep (loses functions/dates)

Functions are first-class citizens in JS. Understanding scope, closures, and 'this' is what separates juniors from seniors.

Closures — The Most Important Concept

function createCounter() {
  let count = 0; // "closed over" variable
  return {
    increment: () => ++count,
    getCount: () => count,
  };
}

const counter = createCounter();
counter.increment(); // 1
counter.increment(); // 2
counter.getCount();  // 2
// count is NOT accessible from outside — it's private!
A closure is like a backpack. When a function is created, it puts the surrounding variables in its backpack. Even after the outer function returns, the inner function still carries those variables in its backpack.

Classic Interview Trap: Loop + setTimeout

// ❌ BROKEN: prints 3, 3, 3
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100);
}

// ✅ FIX 1: use let (block scoping)
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100);
}

// ✅ FIX 2: IIFE (creates new scope per iteration)
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  ((j) => setTimeout(() => console.log(j), 100))(i);
}

The 'this' Keyword — 5 Rules

// 1. Global context: this = window (or globalThis)
console.log(this); // window

// 2. Object method: this = the object
const user = {
  name: 'Alice',
  greet() { console.log(this.name); } // 'Alice'
};

// 3. Arrow function: this = inherited from parent scope (lexical)
const user2 = {
  name: 'Bob',
  greet: () => console.log(this.name) // undefined! (inherits from global)
};

// 4. Explicit binding: call, apply, bind
function greet(greeting) { console.log(`${greeting}, ${this.name}`); }
greet.call({ name: 'Alice' }, 'Hi');     // 'Hi, Alice'
greet.apply({ name: 'Bob' }, ['Hey']);     // 'Hey, Bob'
const bound = greet.bind({ name: 'Eve' });  // returns new function
bound('Hello');                              // 'Hello, Eve'

// 5. new keyword: this = new empty object
function Person(name) { this.name = name; }
const p = new Person('Alice'); // this = {} → { name: 'Alice' }
Interview shortcut: Arrow functions DON'T have their own this. Regular functions DO. This is why arrow functions are great for callbacks (they inherit this from the component) but terrible for object methods.

Higher-Order Functions & Functional Patterns

// A function that takes or returns another function
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

// map — transform each element
nums.map(n => n * 2);         // [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]

// filter — keep only matching
nums.filter(n => n > 3);       // [4, 5]

// reduce — accumulate into single value
nums.reduce((sum, n) => sum + n, 0); // 15

// find — first match (or undefined)
nums.find(n => n > 3);         // 4

// some/every — boolean checks
nums.some(n => n > 4);         // true (at least one)
nums.every(n => n > 0);        // true (all match)

// flatMap — map + flatten one level
[[1,2],[3,4]].flatMap(x => x); // [1, 2, 3, 4]

// Chaining
const result = users
  .filter(u => u.active)
  .map(u => u.name)
  .sort();

Async JavaScript is where interviews get hard. Master promises, async/await, and error handling.

Promises — The Complete Picture

// A Promise has 3 states: pending → fulfilled OR rejected

// Creating
const promise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
  setTimeout(() => resolve('done!'), 1000);
});

// Consuming
promise
  .then(result => console.log(result))   // 'done!'
  .catch(error => console.error(error))  // handle rejection
  .finally(() => console.log('cleanup')); // always runs

// Static methods (INTERVIEW FAVORITES)
Promise.all([p1, p2, p3])      // waits for ALL, fails on FIRST rejection
Promise.allSettled([p1, p2])  // waits for ALL, never rejects, returns status
Promise.race([p1, p2])        // returns FIRST to settle (resolve or reject)
Promise.any([p1, p2])         // returns FIRST to resolve (ignores rejections)
Promise.all = "I need ALL of you to finish. If anyone fails, the whole trip is cancelled." Promise.allSettled = "Everyone finish at your own pace, I'll check on all of you." Promise.race = "First one done wins, I don't care about the rest." Promise.any = "First one to SUCCEED wins, I'll ignore failures."

async/await — Clean Async Code

// async functions ALWAYS return a Promise
async function fetchUser(id) {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);
    return await res.json();
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Failed:', err.message);
    throw err; // re-throw for caller to handle
  }
}

// Parallel execution with await
const [users, posts] = await Promise.all([
  fetchUsers(),
  fetchPosts(),
]); // Both run simultaneously!

// Sequential (when order matters)
const user = await fetchUser(1);
const posts = await fetchPostsByUser(user.id);
await in a forEach does NOT work as expected — use for...of for sequential or Promise.all(arr.map(...)) for parallel.

JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance, not classical. Understanding this is crucial for interviews.

Prototypal Inheritance

// Every object has a hidden [[Prototype]] link
const animal = { speak() { console.log('...'); } };
const dog = Object.create(animal);
dog.speak(); // '...' — found on animal via prototype chain

// ES6 Classes (syntactic sugar over prototypes)
class Animal {
  constructor(name) { this.name = name; }
  speak() { console.log(`${this.name} makes a sound`); }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
  constructor(name, breed) {
    super(name); // MUST call super() before using 'this'
    this.breed = breed;
  }
  speak() { console.log(`${this.name} barks`); }
}

// Private fields (ES2022)
class BankAccount {
  #balance = 0; // truly private
  deposit(amount) { this.#balance += amount; }
  get balance() { return this.#balance; }
}
Prototypes are like a family tree. When you ask an object for a property, it checks itself first. If not found, it asks its parent (prototype), then grandparent, all the way up to Object.prototype (the root ancestor). The first one who has it, provides it.

DOM manipulation and event handling — the bridge between JavaScript and what users see.

Event Delegation & Bubbling

// Events BUBBLE UP from the target to the root
// Click on 
  •  → bubbles to 
       →  →  → document

// Event delegation: ONE listener handles many children
document.querySelector('#list').addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  if (e.target.matches('li')) {
    console.log('Clicked:', e.target.textContent);
  }
});
// Works for dynamically added 
    •  too!

// Stop bubbling
e.stopPropagation();     // stop going up
e.preventDefault();      // prevent default action (form submit, link navigate)
e.stopImmediatePropagation(); // stop all handlers on this element too

// Capture phase (reverse: root → target)
el.addEventListener('click', handler, { capture: true });
    • Bubbling = dropping a stone in water. Ripples go outward (child → parent). Capturing = the opposite: waves come inward (parent → child) before the stone hits.

    Debounce & Throttle

    // Debounce: wait until user STOPS doing something
function debounce(fn, delay) {
  let timer;
  return (...args) => {
    clearTimeout(timer);
    timer = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), delay);
  };
}
// Use case: search input — don't fire API call on every keystroke

// Throttle: execute at most once per interval
function throttle(fn, limit) {
  let inThrottle;
  return (...args) => {
    if (!inThrottle) {
      fn(...args);
      inThrottle = true;
      setTimeout(() => inThrottle = false, limit);
    }
  };
}
// Use case: scroll handler — don't fire 60 times per second

    Modern JavaScript features you must know for any interview.

    Destructuring, Spread, Rest

    // Object destructuring
const { name, age, role = 'user' } = user; // with default
const { name: userName } = user;             // rename
const { address: { city } } = user;          // nested

// Array destructuring
const [first, , third] = [1, 2, 3];         // skip second
const [head, ...tail] = [1, 2, 3];          // rest: tail = [2, 3]

// Spread — expand iterable
const merged = { ...obj1, ...obj2 };         // merge objects (obj2 wins)
const copy = [...arr];                       // shallow copy array
myFunc(...args);                              // spread into arguments

// Rest — collect remaining
function sum(...nums) { return nums.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0); }
const { id, ...rest } = user; // rest has everything except id

    Optional Chaining, Nullish Coalescing & More

    // Optional chaining (?.) — short-circuits to undefined
user?.address?.city           // safe nested access
user?.getName?.()             // safe method call
arr?.[0]                      // safe array access

// Nullish coalescing (??) — only null/undefined trigger fallback
const val = input ?? 'default'; // "" and 0 are NOT null, so they pass through
const val2 = input || 'default'; // "" and 0 ARE falsy, so fallback triggers

// Logical assignment operators
x ??= 5;    // x = x ?? 5 (assign if null/undefined)
x ||= 5;    // x = x || 5 (assign if falsy)
x &&= fn(); // x = x && fn() (call fn only if x is truthy)

// Object.groupBy (ES2024)
const grouped = Object.groupBy(users, u => u.role);
// { admin: [...], user: [...] }

// Promise.withResolvers (ES2024)
const { promise, resolve, reject } = Promise.withResolvers();

// Array.at() — negative indexing
[1,2,3].at(-1); // 3 (last element)

// structuredClone — deep copy
const deep = structuredClone(complexObj);

    Generators, Iterators & Symbols

    // Generator function — pauses execution with yield
function* idGenerator() {
  let id = 1;
  while (true) yield id++;
}
const gen = idGenerator();
gen.next(); // { value: 1, done: false }
gen.next(); // { value: 2, done: false }

// Symbol — unique, immutable identifier
const id = Symbol('id');
const obj = { [id]: 123 };
// Not visible in for...in, Object.keys(), JSON.stringify()

// Well-known symbols
Symbol.iterator  // makes objects iterable with for...of
Symbol.toPrimitive // customize type coercion

    Know these data structures and their performance characteristics.

    Map, Set, WeakMap, WeakRef

    // Map — key-value pairs (any type as key)
const map = new Map();
map.set(userObj, 'data'); // objects as keys!
map.get(userObj);          // 'data'
map.has(userObj);          // true
map.size;                  // 1

// Set — unique values only
const unique = [...new Set([1,2,2,3])]; // [1, 2, 3]

// WeakMap — keys are weakly held (garbage-collectible)
// Use for: private data, caching without memory leaks
const wm = new WeakMap();
wm.set(domElement, { clicks: 0 }); // GC'd when element is removed

// WeakRef — weak reference to an object
const ref = new WeakRef(largeObj);
ref.deref(); // returns object or undefined if GC'd

    TypeScript Fundamentals

    Extending JavaScript with static typing to build robust, scalable applications.

    Types vs Interfaces

    Both are used to define custom structures in TypeScript, but they have subtle differences.

    • Interface: Strictly for object shapes. Can be merged via "Declaration Merging" (if you declare the same interface twice, TS combines their properties). Ideal for public API definitions.
    • Type Aliases (`type`): More flexible. Can define primitives, unions, intersections, and mapped types. Cannot be declaration-merged. Ideal for internal component definitions and complex utility types.
    // Interface (Object Shape)
interface User { id: number; name: string; }

// Type (Union)
type Status = "idle" | "loading" | "success" | "error";

// Intersection (Combining shapes)
type AdminUser = User & { role: "admin" | "superadmin" };
    Generics & Utility Types

    Generics allow you to write reusable code that works across multiple types.

    • `T` as an Argument: Think of a generic `` like a type variable passed into a function.
    • Partial<T>: Makes all properties in T optional. Useful for update/patch payloads.
    • Omit<T, K>: Creates a type by removing specific keys `K` from `T`.
    • Record<K, T>: Creates an object type where keys are of type `K` and values are of type `T`.
    // Generic Function: infers type T from the input array
function getFirstItem<T>(items: T[]): T | undefined {
  return items[0];
}

const num = getFirstItem([1, 2, 3]); // Hover: num is number
const str = getFirstItem(["a", "b"]); // Hover: str is string
    Discriminated Unions (Tagged Unions)

    A pattern used to safely exhaust all possible variations of a type by sharing a single common literal property (the "discriminator").

    type Action = 
  | { type: "LOADING" } 
  | { type: "SUCCESS"; payload: string[] } 
  | { type: "ERROR"; errorMsg: string };

function reducer(action: Action) {
  // TypeScript knows exactly what properties are available
  // based on the 'type' property.
  switch (action.type) {
    case "LOADING":
      return 'Loading...';
    case "SUCCESS":
      return action.payload.length; // Safe to access payload
    case "ERROR":
      return action.errorMsg; // Safe to access errorMsg
  }
}

    Modern JS & Web APIs

    Harnessing the latest ECMAScript features and modern browser APIs.

    ECMAScript 2024+ (ES15)
    • Object.groupBy / Map.groupBy: Natively groups an array of objects into an object (or Map) based on a string returned by a callback. Replaces the need for Lodash `_.groupBy`.
    • Promise.withResolvers: Returns an object containing a new Promise and its `resolve` and `reject` functions. Fixes the old awkward pattern of extracting resolvers from the Promise constructor.
    • Array.prototype.with: Safe array mutation. `arr.with(index, value)` returns a *new* array with the element at `index` replaced by `value`, leaving the original array intact. Great for React/Redux!
    • Temporal API (Draft): The modern replacement for the broken `Date` object, providing full timezone awareness, calendar math, and immutable date objects.
    // Example of Promise.withResolvers
const { promise, resolve, reject } = Promise.withResolvers();

setTimeout(() => resolve("Done!"), 1000);
promise.then(console.log); // "Done!" after 1s
    Modern Web APIs
    • IntersectionObserver: Asynchronously observe changes in the intersection of a target element with an ancestor or the viewport. Crucial for lazy-loading images and infinite scrolling without hurting performance via massive scroll event listeners.
    • ResizeObserver: Reports changes to the dimensions of an element's content or border box. Provides a performant way to build truly responsive elements independently of the viewport (container queries in JS).
    • MutationObserver: Watches the DOM tree for changes (nodes added/removed, attributes modified) and fires a callback. Used internally by frameworks, but vital for writing extensions or observing 3rd party injects.
    • Web Workers: Runs scripts in background threads. The worker thread can perform computations without interfering with the user interface (main thread). Cannot access the DOM directly.
    JS Design Patterns
    • Singleton: Ensures a class has only one single instance and provides a global access point to it (e.g., establishing a single database connection or global store).
    • Observer / Pub-Sub: A subject maintains a list of dependents (observers) and notifies them automatically of state changes (e.g., Redux, RxJS Subjects, DOM Event listeners).
    • Factory: A function or method that creates objects without specifying the exact class of object that will be created. Simplifies complex object initialization.
    • Proxy: The modern ES6 `Proxy` object lets you wrap an object and intercept fundamental operations (reading/writing properties). Used extensively by Vue 3 and Zustand to track reactive state dependencies.

    JavaScript interview questions from beginner to advanced. Click to reveal answers.

    Showing 0 questions
    BWhat is the difference between == and ===?+

    == compares values after type coercion. === compares values AND types with no coercion. Always use === to avoid unexpected behavior like "5" == 5 being true.

    BWhat are the differences between var, let, and const?+

    var: function-scoped, hoisted as undefined, can redeclare. let: block-scoped, hoisted but in TDZ (Temporal Dead Zone), can reassign. const: block-scoped, must initialize, cannot reassign (but objects/arrays can be mutated). Default to const, use let when reassignment is needed, never use var.

    BWhat is hoisting?+

    JavaScript moves declarations to the top of their scope before execution. var is hoisted and initialized as undefined. let/const are hoisted but NOT initialized (accessing them before declaration throws a ReferenceError — the Temporal Dead Zone). Function declarations are fully hoisted (you can call them before they appear in code). Function expressions are NOT.

    IExplain closures with an example.+

    A closure is a function that remembers variables from its outer scope even after that scope has finished executing. Example: function counter() { let n = 0; return () => ++n; } — the returned function "closes over" n, keeping it alive. Use cases: data privacy, factory functions, memoization, event handlers.

    IWhat is the event loop? Explain the execution order.+

    The event loop manages async operations in single-threaded JS. Order: 1) Execute all synchronous code on the call stack. 2) Process ALL microtasks (Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). 3) Process ONE macro task (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O). 4) Repeat. Microtasks always run before the next macro task.

    Like a restaurant: finish all current orders (sync), then check the VIP queue (microtasks), then check the regular queue (macrotasks), repeat.
    IExplain 'this' in JavaScript.+

    this depends on HOW a function is called: 1) Global: window. 2) Object method: the object. 3) Arrow function: inherited from parent (lexical). 4) call/apply/bind: explicitly set. 5) new: the new object. Arrow functions are the key difference — they DON'T have their own this.

    IWhat is the difference between Promise.all, allSettled, race, and any?+

    all: waits for ALL to resolve, rejects on first failure. allSettled: waits for ALL regardless, returns array of {status, value/reason}. race: returns first to settle (resolve or reject). any: returns first to resolve, only rejects if ALL reject (AggregateError).

    AExplain prototypal inheritance and the prototype chain.+

    Every JS object has a hidden [[Prototype]] link to another object. When accessing a property, JS walks up this chain: object → its prototype → that prototype's prototype → … → Object.prototype → null. Classes are syntactic sugar — extends sets up the chain, super() calls the parent constructor. Object.create(proto) creates an object with proto as its prototype.

    AWhat is event delegation and why is it useful?+

    Instead of attaching listeners to each child element, attach ONE listener to a parent and use event.target to determine which child was clicked. Benefits: works for dynamically added elements, uses less memory, fewer listeners to manage. Uses event bubbling — events propagate from target up to ancestors.

    AImplement debounce from scratch.+

    function debounce(fn, delay) { let timer; return (...args) => { clearTimeout(timer); timer = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), delay); }; } — Uses closures to persist the timer. Each call resets the timer. The function only fires after the user stops triggering it for the specified delay. Real use: search inputs, window resize handlers.

    AWhat are WeakMap and WeakRef? When would you use them?+

    WeakMap: Like Map but keys must be objects and are weakly held — if nothing else references the key, it can be garbage collected. Use for: private data associated with DOM elements, caching without memory leaks. WeakRef: Holds a weak reference to an object. deref() returns the object or undefined if GC'd. Use for: caches where you don't want to prevent garbage collection.

    IWhat is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?+

    The period between entering a scope and the actual declaration of a let/const variable. During the TDZ, accessing the variable throws a ReferenceError. This exists to catch bugs where you accidentally use a variable before it's declared.

    BWhat are arrow functions? How do they differ from regular functions?+

    Arrow functions have shorter syntax (() => {}), no own this (inherit from parent), no arguments object, cannot be used as constructors (no new), and no prototype property. Use them for callbacks and inline functions. Use regular functions for methods and constructors.

    IWhat is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy?+

    Shallow copy: copies only the first level. Nested objects still share references. Methods: spread {...obj}, Object.assign(). Deep copy: copies all levels recursively. Methods: structuredClone() (modern, recommended), JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) (loses functions, dates, undefined).

    AExplain generators and their use cases.+

    Generator functions (function*) can pause and resume execution via yield. Calling a generator returns an iterator with .next(). Use cases: lazy evaluation of infinite sequences, implementing custom iterators, controlling async flow (though async/await is preferred now), state machines.

    AWhat is currying? Implement it.+

    Currying transforms f(a, b, c) into f(a)(b)(c). Each call returns a new function until all arguments are collected. Implementation: const curry = (fn) => { const arity = fn.length; return function curried(...args) { return args.length >= arity ? fn(...args) : (...more) => curried(...args, ...more); }; }. Use cases: partial application, creating reusable function factories.

    React 19 is the current stable version. The mental model has shifted: server-first, compiler-optimized, less useEffect.

    What React Is

    React is a JavaScript library (not framework) for building UIs with components. It uses a Virtual DOM to efficiently update the real DOM. React 19 brings Server Components, the React Compiler, Actions, and a new mental model where the compiler handles most optimization automatically.

    React is like a recipe system. You describe WHAT the UI should look like for given data (declarative). React figures out HOW to efficiently update the screen (reconciliation).

    JSX — It's Just JavaScript

    // JSX compiles to React.createElement() calls
const element = <h1 className="title">Hello, {name}!</h1>;

// Rules of JSX:
// 1. Return a single root element (use <> Fragment if needed)
// 2. Close all tags (including , 
    )    
// 3. camelCase for attributes (className, onClick, htmlFor)
// 4. Expressions in {} — any valid JS expression

// Conditional rendering
{isLoggedIn && <Welcome />}
{isLoggedIn ? <Dashboard /> : <Login />}

// List rendering
{items.map(item => <li key={item.id}>{item.name}</li>)}

// Spreading props
<Button {...buttonProps} />
    key is required on list items. Use a stable, unique ID — never use array index as key (causes bugs with reordering, deletion). React uses keys to track which items changed.

    Components — Function Components Only

    // Class components are legacy. Use function components always.

// Basic component
function Greeting({ name, age = 25 }) {
  return <p>Hello, {name}! You are {age}.</p>;
}

// With TypeScript
interface Props {
  name: string;
  age?: number;
  onSave: (data: FormData) => void;
  children: React.ReactNode;
}

function UserCard({ name, age = 25, onSave, children }: Props) {
  return (
    <div>
      <h2>{name}, {age}</h2>
      {children}
      <button onClick={() => onSave(formData)}>Save</button>
    </div>
  );
}

    State & Props — Data Flow

    // Props flow DOWN (parent → child). Read-only.
// State is LOCAL. Triggers re-render when changed.

function Counter() {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  // ❌ WRONG: stale state
  // setCount(count + 1); setCount(count + 1); → only increments by 1

  // ✅ CORRECT: functional update
  const increment = () => setCount(prev => prev + 1);
  // setCount(p => p + 1); setCount(p => p + 1); → increments by 2

  return <button onClick={increment}>Count: {count}</button>;
}

// State update rules:
// 1. State updates are BATCHED (async)
// 2. State is IMMUTABLE — never mutate directly
// 3. Use functional updates for derived state

// Immutable updates
setItems(prev => [...prev, newItem]);              // add
setItems(prev => prev.filter(i => i.id !== id));   // remove
setItems(prev => prev.map(i =>                     // update
  i.id === id ? { ...i, name: 'new' } : i
));
    Props are like arguments passed to a function. State is like a variable inside the function that React tracks. When state changes, React "calls" the function again (re-renders) with the new state.

    Hooks are how you add state, effects, context, and more to function components.

    Every Hook You Need

    // useState — reactive state
const [state, setState] = useState(initialValue);
const [state, setState] = useState(() => expensiveCompute()); // lazy init

// useEffect — side effects (API calls, subscriptions, DOM manipulation)
useEffect(() => {
  const sub = subscribe();
  return () => sub.unsubscribe(); // cleanup on unmount/re-run
}, [dependency]); // only re-run when dependency changes
// [] = run once on mount. No array = run every render.

// useRef — mutable reference that persists across renders
const inputRef = useRef(null);
inputRef.current.focus(); // access DOM element
const renderCount = useRef(0); // mutable value that doesn't trigger re-render

// useMemo — memoize expensive computations
const sorted = useMemo(() => items.sort(compareFn), [items]);

// useCallback — memoize functions
const handleClick = useCallback(() => {
  doSomething(id);
}, [id]);

// useContext — consume context without wrapper
const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);

// useReducer — complex state logic
const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState);

// useId — unique IDs for accessibility
const id = useId(); // "r:1" — works with SSR
    With the React Compiler (React 19), useMemo and useCallback are rarely needed manually. The compiler auto-memoizes for you. Still learn them for interviews, but don't overuse them in new code.

    React 19 Hooks

    // useActionState — manage form/action state
const [state, formAction, isPending] = useActionState(
  async (prevState, formData) => {
    const result = await submitForm(formData);
    return result;
  },
  initialState
);

// useOptimistic — optimistic UI updates
const [optimisticItems, addOptimistic] = useOptimistic(
  items,
  (current, newItem) => [...current, { ...newItem, pending: true }]
);

// useTransition — mark non-urgent updates
const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();
startTransition(async () => {
  await updateData(); // React 19: async transitions!
});

// use() — read resources in render (Promises, Context)
function UserProfile({ userPromise }) {
  const user = use(userPromise); // suspends until resolved
  return <h1>{user.name}</h1>;
}

    Patterns that make React apps maintainable and scalable.

    State Management Spectrum

    Level 1: useState + prop drilling

    Fine for small apps with shallow component trees.

    Level 2: useContext + useReducer

    Good for medium apps. Context provides global access; useReducer handles complex state transitions.

    Level 3: Zustand / Jotai

    Lightweight external stores. Zustand for one global store; Jotai for atomic state. Both are React-first and simple.

    Level 4: TanStack Query (React Query)

    For server state (API data). Handles caching, refetching, optimistic updates, pagination. The best solution for data fetching state.

    Level 5: Redux Toolkit

    For very complex client state in large enterprise apps. Full predictability with time-travel debugging. Overkill for most apps.

    In 2026, TanStack Query + Zustand is the most popular combo. Use TanStack Query for server state (API data) and Zustand for client state (UI state like modals, theme, sidebar open).

    Custom Hooks — Reusable Logic

    // Extract reusable stateful logic
function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay: number): T {
  const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);
  useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [value, delay]);
  return debounced;
}

function useLocalStorage<T>(key: string, initial: T) {
  const [value, setValue] = useState(() => {
    const stored = localStorage.getItem(key);
    return stored ? JSON.parse(stored) : initial;
  });
  useEffect(() => {
    localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify(value));
  }, [key, value]);
  return [value, setValue] as const;
}

// Usage
const debouncedSearch = useDebounce(searchTerm, 300);
const [theme, setTheme] = useLocalStorage('theme', 'dark');

    Composition Patterns

    // 1. Children pattern (most common)
<Card>
  <h2>Title</h2>
  <p>Content</p>
</Card>

// 2. Render props
<DataFetcher url="/api/users" render={(data) => <UserList users={data} />} />

// 3. Compound components (like HTML select/option)
<Tabs>
  <Tabs.List>
    <Tabs.Tab>Profile</Tabs.Tab>
    <Tabs.Tab>Settings</Tabs.Tab>
  </Tabs.List>
  <Tabs.Panel>Profile content</Tabs.Panel>
  <Tabs.Panel>Settings content</Tabs.Panel>
</Tabs>

// 4. Higher-Order Component (legacy, use hooks instead)
const withAuth = (Component) => (props) => {
  const user = useAuth();
  if (!user) return <Redirect to="/login" />;
  return <Component {...props} user={user} />;
};

    React 19 is a paradigm shift. Server Components, the Compiler, and Actions change everything.

    React Compiler (React Forget)

    The React Compiler automatically memoizes components and values at build time. This means useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo are rarely needed manually.

    // Before (React 18): manual memoization
const sorted = useMemo(() => items.sort(), [items]);
const handleClick = useCallback(() => save(id), [id]);

// After (React 19 + Compiler): just write normal code
const sorted = items.sort(); // compiler handles memoization
const handleClick = () => save(id); // compiler handles it

    Server Components (RSC)

    Components that run exclusively on the server. They can access databases, file systems, and APIs directly. Their code is NEVER sent to the client.

    // Server Component (default in Next.js App Router)
async function UserList() {
  const users = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users'); // direct DB access!
  return (
    <ul>
      {users.map(u => <li key={u.id}>{u.name}</li>)}
    </ul>
  );
}

// Client Component (opt-in with 'use client')
'use client';
function LikeButton() {
  const [liked, setLiked] = useState(false);
  return <button onClick={() => setLiked(!liked)}>{liked ? '❤️' : '🤍'}</button>;
}
    Server Components are like a kitchen in a restaurant. The cooking (data fetching, heavy logic) happens in the kitchen (server). Only the plated dish (HTML) goes to the customer (browser). Client Components are like the table-side experience — interactive things the customer does themselves (clicking buttons, filling forms).

    Server Actions & Forms

    // Server Action — runs on the server, called from client
'use server';
async function createUser(formData: FormData) {
  const name = formData.get('name');
  await db.insert('users', { name });
  revalidatePath('/users');
}

// Used in a form
<form action={createUser}>
  <input name="name" />
  <button type="submit">Create</button>
</form>

// With useActionState for loading/error states
const [state, action, isPending] = useActionState(createUser, null);
<form action={action}>
  <button disabled={isPending}>
    {isPending ? 'Saving...' : 'Create'}
  </button>
</form>

    Performance is about avoiding unnecessary re-renders and reducing bundle size.

    Re-render Rules

    A component re-renders when: 1) Its state changes. 2) Its parent re-renders (even if props haven't changed). 3) Context value it consumes changes.

    Optimization Strategies

    // 1. Move state down (closer to where it's used)
// 2. Lift content up (children as props pattern)
// 3. React.memo — skip re-render if props unchanged
const MemoizedChild = React.memo(ExpensiveChild);

// 4. Lazy loading — split code by route/component
const HeavyComponent = lazy(() => import('./HeavyComponent'));
<Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
  <HeavyComponent />
</Suspense>

// 5. Virtualization for long lists
// Use @tanstack/react-virtual or react-window

// 6. useTransition for non-urgent updates
const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();
startTransition(() => setFilteredList(filter(items)));

    React interview questions from beginner to advanced.

    Showing 0 questions
    BWhat is the Virtual DOM?+

    A lightweight JavaScript copy of the real DOM. When state changes, React creates a new virtual DOM, diffs it with the previous one (reconciliation), and updates only the changed parts of the real DOM. This is faster than manipulating the DOM directly.

    Like editing a Word document: you make changes in a draft (virtual DOM), then only print the pages that changed (real DOM updates).
    BWhat are React hooks? Name the main ones.+

    Functions that let you use React features in function components. Main hooks: useState (state), useEffect (side effects), useContext (context), useRef (mutable refs), useMemo (memoize values), useCallback (memoize functions), useReducer (complex state), useId (unique IDs). Rules: only call at the top level, only in React functions.

    IWhat is the useEffect cleanup function for?+

    The function returned from useEffect runs before the component unmounts or before the effect re-runs. Use it to: unsubscribe from subscriptions, remove event listeners, cancel timers, abort fetch requests. Without cleanup, you get memory leaks and stale state bugs.

    IWhat is the difference between useMemo and useCallback?+

    useMemo memoizes the return value of a computation. useCallback memoizes the function itself. useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). With the React 19 Compiler, both are rarely needed manually.

    IExplain React Context and its limitations.+

    Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without prop drilling. Create with createContext(), provide with <Context.Provider>, consume with useContext(). Limitations: any component consuming context re-renders when the value changes, even if it only uses a small part. For frequent updates, use external state (Zustand, Jotai) instead.

    AWhat are React Server Components? How do they differ from SSR?+

    SSR: Renders the entire app on the server as HTML, then "hydrates" it on the client (full JS bundle still sent). RSC: Server Components run ONLY on the server, their JS is never sent to the client. They can access databases directly, have zero bundle size impact, and render alongside Client Components. The key difference: SSR still sends all component code to the client; RSC doesn't.

    AWhat is the React Compiler and how does it change development?+

    The React Compiler (previously "React Forget") is a build-time tool that automatically memoizes components and values. It analyses your code and inserts useMemo/useCallback equivalents where needed. This means: fewer re-renders without manual optimization, less code to write and maintain, and useMemo/useCallback become last-resort tools rather than defaults.

    AExplain React's reconciliation algorithm.+

    When state changes, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and compares it with the previous one (diffing). The algorithm: 1) Compare root elements — different types = replace entire subtree. 2) Same type = update attributes and recurse into children. 3) Lists use keys to match children across renders. This gives O(n) performance instead of the O(n³) naive tree diff. That's why keys are critical for list performance.

    IWhat causes unnecessary re-renders and how do you prevent them?+

    Causes: parent re-renders (all children re-render by default), new object/array references in props, context value changes. Prevention: 1) React.memo for pure components. 2) Move state down closer to where it's used. 3) Lift content up (children as props). 4) Use the React Compiler (auto-memoization). 5) Split context into smaller pieces. 6) Use external state management for frequently changing values.

    AWhat are Server Actions in React 19?+

    Server Actions are async functions marked with 'use server' that run on the server but can be called from client components. They're used for data mutations (form submissions, database writes). They work with the <form action={...}> pattern, support progressive enhancement (work without JS), and integrate with useActionState for pending/error states.

    Next.js 16.2 is the latest (March 2026). It's a full-stack React framework with App Router, Server Components, and Turbopack.

    What Next.js Is

    Next.js is a React framework by Vercel that adds: file-based routing, server-side rendering (SSR), static site generation (SSG), API routes, Server Actions, middleware, image optimization, and built-in CSS/Tailwind support. It's the recommended way to build production React apps in 2026.

    App Router — File-Based Routing

    app/
├── layout.tsx          # Root layout (wraps all pages)
├── page.tsx            # Home page (/)
├── loading.tsx         # Loading UI (auto Suspense boundary)
├── error.tsx           # Error boundary
├── not-found.tsx       # 404 page
├── about/
│   └── page.tsx        # /about
├── blog/
│   ├── page.tsx        # /blog
│   └── [slug]/
│       └── page.tsx    # /blog/:slug (dynamic route)
├── dashboard/
│   ├── layout.tsx      # Nested layout
│   ├── page.tsx        # /dashboard
│   └── settings/
│       └── page.tsx    # /dashboard/settings
└── api/
    └── users/
        └── route.ts    # API route: GET/POST /api/users

    Special Files

    page.tsx = the UI for a route. layout.tsx = shared UI that wraps children (persists across navigation). loading.tsx = automatic Suspense fallback. error.tsx = error boundary. not-found.tsx = 404 page. route.ts = API endpoint. template.tsx = like layout but re-mounts on navigation.

    Layouts — Shared, Persistent UI

    // app/layout.tsx — root layout (REQUIRED)
export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <Navbar />
        {children}   {/* page content renders here */}
        <Footer />
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

// app/dashboard/layout.tsx — nested layout
export default function DashLayout({ children }) {
  return (
    <div className="flex">
      <Sidebar />
      <main>{children}</main>
    </div>
  );
}
    Layouts don't re-render on navigation — they persist state. This means a sidebar component with open/close state will maintain its state as you navigate between pages. This is a massive UX win.

    Dynamic Routes & Route Handlers

    // app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx — dynamic route
export default async function BlogPost({ params }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const post = await getPost(slug);
  return <article>{post.title}</article>;
}

// Generate static paths at build time
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const posts = await getAllPosts();
  return posts.map(p => ({ slug: p.slug }));
}

// app/api/users/route.ts — API route handler
export async function GET(request: Request) {
  const users = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users');
  return Response.json(users);
}

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const body = await request.json();
  const user = await db.insert('users', body);
  return Response.json(user, { status: 201 });
}

    Next.js offers the most flexible rendering model of any framework.

    Rendering Strategies

    1. Static (SSG) — Build Time

    Pages rendered at build time. Fastest possible delivery. Use for: blog posts, docs, marketing pages.

    2. Dynamic (SSR) — Request Time

    Pages rendered on every request. Use for: dashboards, user-specific content, search results.

    3. ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration)

    Static pages that revalidate in the background. Use for: e-commerce, CMS-driven content.

    4. Partial Prerendering (PPR) — Next.js 16

    Combines static and dynamic in the SAME page. Static shell is served instantly; dynamic parts stream in via Suspense.

    // Static by default. Becomes dynamic when you use:
// - cookies(), headers(), searchParams
// - Uncached data fetches
// - dynamic = 'force-dynamic'

// ISR — revalidate cached data
const data = await fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 3600 } }); // every hour

// Cache Components (Next.js 16) — explicit caching with 'use cache'
'use cache';
export default async function ProductList() {
  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products');
  return <Grid products={products} />;
}

    Metadata & SEO

    // Static metadata
export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: 'My App',
  description: 'The best app ever',
  openGraph: { images: ['/og-image.png'] },
};

// Dynamic metadata
export async function generateMetadata({ params }): Promise<Metadata> {
  const post = await getPost(params.slug);
  return { title: post.title, description: post.excerpt };
}

    Data fetching and mutations in Next.js 16.

    Server Actions — Zero-API Data Mutations

    // app/actions.ts
'use server';

export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
  const title = formData.get('title') as string;
  const content = formData.get('content') as string;

  await db.insert('posts', { title, content });
  revalidatePath('/posts');  // clear cache for this path
  redirect('/posts');         // navigate after mutation
}

// app/posts/new/page.tsx
import { createPost } from '../actions';

export default function NewPost() {
  return (
    <form action={createPost}>
      <input name="title" required />
      <textarea name="content" required />
      <button type="submit">Publish</button>
    </form>
  );
}
    Server Actions are like having a magic mailbox. You put a form in the mailbox (submit), it goes straight to the server, the server processes it and updates the page — no need to build a separate API endpoint or write fetch() calls.

    Middleware — Request-Level Logic

    // middleware.ts (at project root)
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server';

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const token = request.cookies.get('token');

  if (!token && request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/dashboard')) {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url));
  }

  // Add headers, rewrite URLs, etc.
  const response = NextResponse.next();
  response.headers.set('x-custom', 'value');
  return response;
}

export const config = {
  matcher: ['/dashboard/:path*', '/api/:path*'],
};

    Next.js 16 and 16.2 introduced major changes. Know these for interviews.

    Next.js 16 — Key Features

    • Turbopack stable — Default bundler. Up to 5-10x faster Fast Refresh, 2-5x faster builds.
    • Cache Components & 'use cache' — Explicit, opt-in caching replacing the old implicit caching model. All code is dynamic by default now.
    • Partial Prerendering (PPR) — Static shell + dynamic streaming in the same page.
    • React Compiler support — Built-in integration for automatic memoization.
    • proxy.ts — Replaces middleware for network proxy use cases.
    • Adapter API — Stable API for deploying to any hosting provider (not just Vercel).
    • DevTools MCP — AI agents can interact with Next.js diagnostics.

    Next.js 16.2 — Latest (March 2026)

    • ~400% faster dev startup and ~50% faster rendering
    • Server Fast Refresh — Fine-grained server-side hot reloading
    • AI Improvements — AGENTS.md in create-next-app, browser log forwarding, experimental Agent DevTools
    • SRI support — Subresource Integrity for JavaScript files
    • 200+ Turbopack bug fixes
    The React Server Component payload deserialization is now up to 350% faster in 16.2, translating to 25-60% faster HTML rendering depending on payload size.

    Next.js interview questions.

    Showing 0 questions
    BWhat is Next.js and why use it over plain React?+

    Next.js is a React framework that adds SSR, SSG, file-based routing, API routes, image optimization, and built-in CSS support. Plain React is a library that only handles the UI layer — you need to add routing, SSR, and build tooling yourself. Next.js gives you everything out of the box with sensible defaults.

    BWhat is the App Router vs Pages Router?+

    App Router (recommended): Uses the app/ directory. Supports Server Components, layouts, loading states, streaming. Pages Router (legacy): Uses pages/ directory. All components are Client Components. Uses getServerSideProps/getStaticProps for data fetching. New projects should always use App Router.

    IExplain the different rendering strategies in Next.js.+

    SSG: HTML generated at build time. Fastest. For static content. SSR: HTML generated per request. For dynamic/personalised content. ISR: Static pages that revalidate in background. Best of both worlds. PPR: Static shell served instantly, dynamic parts stream in. Next.js 16's most powerful feature. CSR: Traditional client-side rendering with 'use client' components.

    IWhat are Server Components vs Client Components in Next.js?+

    Server Components (default): Run on server, zero JS sent to client, can access databases directly. Client Components: Marked with 'use client', include interactivity (useState, onClick, etc.), sent as JS to browser. Rule: keep components as Server Components unless they need interactivity. Client Components can render Server Components as children.

    AWhat is Partial Prerendering (PPR)?+

    PPR combines static and dynamic rendering in the same page. The static parts (header, layout, cached content) are prerendered and served instantly from CDN. Dynamic parts (user-specific data, real-time info) are wrapped in Suspense boundaries and streamed as they become ready. This gives you CDN-speed initial loads with fully dynamic content — no compromise.

    AWhat is 'use cache' in Next.js 16 and how does it differ from the old caching model?+

    In Next.js 15, caching was implicit — fetch requests were cached by default, which confused many developers. Next.js 16 makes caching entirely opt-in with the 'use cache' directive. All code is dynamic by default. You explicitly mark pages, components, or functions with 'use cache' to cache them. The compiler auto-generates cache keys. This is more predictable and aligns with developer expectations.

    AWhat is the Adapter API in Next.js 16?+

    The Adapter API is a stable interface that allows any hosting provider to deploy Next.js applications. Previously, advanced features like ISR and middleware worked best on Vercel. Now, providers like Cloudflare, AWS, and Netlify can implement the adapter interface to support the full feature set. This makes Next.js truly platform-agnostic.

    IWhat is middleware in Next.js?+

    Middleware runs BEFORE a request is completed. It can redirect, rewrite URLs, set headers, or modify the response. Defined in middleware.ts at the project root. Runs on the Edge Runtime (lightweight, fast). Use cases: authentication checks, geo-based redirects, A/B testing, bot detection.

    Angular 21 — Quick Reference

    Current Version: Angular 21 (November 2025, active support). Angular 22 expected May 2026.

    What's New in v21

    • Signal Forms (experimental) — Reactive forms powered by signals, replacing RxJS-based forms
    • Zoneless by default — No more Zone.js for new projects
    • Vitest — Default test runner replacing Karma
    • @angular/aria — Accessible component library (developer preview)
    • MCP Server — AI-assisted development via ng mcp
    • HttpClient by default — No manual setup needed
    • All signals stable — signal(), computed(), effect(), linkedSignal(), signal inputs/queries

    Key Concepts to Master

    Components · Templates · Services · DI · RxJS · Routing · Forms · Signals · Change Detection · Lifecycle Hooks · Pipes · Directives · Interceptors · Guards · SSR/Hydration · @defer · OnPush

                  + Reactive Forms  -form, formik)
